source_title: Knotless single- vs double-row construct design
topic: Knotless single- vs double-row construct design
summary: Knotless constructs eliminate medial-row knot tying, reducing knot impingement and procedure steps. In a SutureBridge configuration, knotless medial rows actually show a greater load-dependent 'self-reinforcement' effect than knotted medial rows, with no loss of ultimate failure load or footprint contact in cadaveric testing. The trade-off debated in the literature is that a fully knotless medial row, if the lateral anchor pulls or the tape cuts, can lose all medial fixation (no independent medial knot as a backstop). All three vendors sell both knotted and knotless options.
arthrex_position: SpeedBridge is fully knotless (FiberTape bridged into SwiveLock anchors, no medial knots); Arthrex also offers knotted SutureBridge and a knotless FiberTak SpeedBridge SP for surgeons who want soft medial anchors.
stryker_position: ReelX STT and Omega are knotless anchors; the reeling mechanism is marketed as letting the surgeon incrementally tension a knotless repair, addressing the common critique that knotless repairs lock in tension too early.
smithnephew_position: HEALICOIL KNOTLESS is S&N's knotless lateral anchor used in ULTRALOCK-type constructs; the company frames knotless as part of a streamlined, healing-focused workflow rather than purely a mechanical claim.
rep_talk_track: Knotless reps (any brand) honestly emphasize fewer steps, no bulky medial knot stack, and biomechanical data showing knotless medial rows self-reinforce well. A rep favoring a knotted or hybrid construct can fairly raise the counterpoint that an independent medial knot provides a fixation backstop and may matter in poor tissue. Stryker's distinctive honest pitch is incremental intra-op tensioning via the ReelX dial. None of these knotless-vs-knotted distinctions is backed by clear clinical-outcome superiority in RCTs - it is biomechanics plus workflow preference.
evidence: Cadaveric study: knotless TOE improved biomechanical self-reinforcement vs medial knotted repair without diminishing footprint contact; no difference in ultimate failure load or stiffness.
Systematic review on tying the medial row: mixed/biomechanical evidence; no definitive clinical-outcome winner between knotted and knotless medial rows.
text:
Knotless single- vs double-row construct design

Knotless constructs eliminate medial-row knot tying, reducing knot impingement and procedure steps. In a SutureBridge configuration, knotless medial rows actually show a greater load-dependent 'self-reinforcement' effect than knotted medial rows, with no loss of ultimate failure load or footprint contact in cadaveric testing. The trade-off debated in the literature is that a fully knotless medial row, if the lateral anchor pulls or the tape cuts, can lose all medial fixation (no independent medial knot as a backstop). All three vendors sell both knotted and knotless options.

Arthrex: SpeedBridge is fully knotless (FiberTape bridged into SwiveLock anchors, no medial knots); Arthrex also offers knotted SutureBridge and a knotless FiberTak SpeedBridge SP for surgeons who want soft medial anchors.
Stryker: ReelX STT and Omega are knotless anchors; the reeling mechanism is marketed as letting the surgeon incrementally tension a knotless repair, addressing the common critique that knotless repairs lock in tension too early.
Smith & Nephew: HEALICOIL KNOTLESS is S&N's knotless lateral anchor used in ULTRALOCK-type constructs; the company frames knotless as part of a streamlined, healing-focused workflow rather than purely a mechanical claim.

Rep positioning: Knotless reps (any brand) honestly emphasize fewer steps, no bulky medial knot stack, and biomechanical data showing knotless medial rows self-reinforce well. A rep favoring a knotted or hybrid construct can fairly raise the counterpoint that an independent medial knot provides a fixation backstop and may matter in poor tissue. Stryker's distinctive honest pitch is incremental intra-op tensioning via the ReelX dial. None of these knotless-vs-knotted distinctions is backed by clear clinical-outcome superiority in RCTs - it is biomechanics plus workflow preference.

Evidence: (1) Cadaveric study: knotless TOE improved biomechanical self-reinforcement vs medial knotted repair without diminishing footprint contact; no difference in ultimate failure load or stiffness. (2) Systematic review on tying the medial row: mixed/biomechanical evidence; no definitive clinical-outcome winner between knotted and knotless medial rows.
